name: Platform‑Neutral Access Mandate
consequences: Immediate: Enforce procurement clauses requiring platform‑neutral authentication → Systemic: Reduce dependency on Apple/Google by 30% within 12 months (measured by % of authentication flows using alternative platforms) → Strategic: Enhances national resilience but may slow deployment speed due to vendor adaptation. Trade‑off: Speed vs. Resilience.
options: Adopt a phased procurement policy that mandates platform‑neutral authentication for all new digital identity services.
Introduce a hybrid procurement model that allows platform‑neutral options alongside existing platforms, with mandatory fallback testing.
Implement a radical open‑source authentication framework that replaces platform APIs with a blockchain‑based trust anchor, enabling fully decentralized fallback paths.
review: Controls Speed vs. Resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs for existing services.

name: Fallback Authentication Demonstrator
consequences: Immediate: Build a non‑production fallback demonstrator using FIDO2/WebAuthn → Systemic: Validate fallback viability, increasing confidence by 25% among stakeholders (measured by stakeholder survey) → Strategic: Provides evidence for policy but may divert resources from core development. Trade‑off: Resource Allocation vs. Evidence Credibility.
options: Develop a lightweight mobile fallback demonstrator using GrapheneOS and FIDO2.
Expand to a browser‑based fallback using WebAuthn and hardware tokens.
Deploy a radical quantum‑resistant fallback using lattice‑based cryptography and distributed ledger verification.
review: Controls Resource Allocation vs. Evidence Credibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider user adoption friction.

name: Coalition Engagement Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Mobilize civil society and academia into a formal coalition → Systemic: Increase policy influence by 40% (measured by number of joint submissions) → Strategic: Strengthens advocacy but may dilute focus. Trade‑off: Broad Influence vs. Focused Action.
options: Form a formal coalition of privacy advocates and civil society groups to co‑author policy briefs.
Expand coalition to include industry stakeholders and security firms for joint research.
Create a radical transnational coalition that partners with EU digital identity bodies to co‑develop a pan‑European fallback standard.
review: Controls Broad Influence vs. Focused Action. Weakness: The options fail to consider coordination overhead.

name: Standards Alignment Initiative
consequences: Immediate: Align project outputs with eIDAS2 and NSIS standards → Systemic: Achieve 80% compliance in AltID procurement documents (measured by standard alignment score) → Strategic: Facilitates integration but may constrain innovation. Trade‑off: Standardization vs. Innovation.
options: Map existing requirements to eIDAS2 and produce compliance checklist.
Propose extensions to eIDAS2 that include platform‑neutral fallback clauses.
Introduce a radical open standards framework that replaces eIDAS2 with a modular, AI‑driven identity verification protocol.
review: Controls Standardization vs. Innovation. Weakness: The options fail to consider legacy system compatibility.

name: Regulatory Impact Assessment
consequences: Immediate: Conduct a regulatory impact assessment of platform‑neutral mandates → Systemic: Reduce regulatory uncertainty by 35% (measured by number of identified compliance gaps) → Strategic: Lowers barriers to adoption but may delay procurement cycles. Trade‑off: Regulatory Clarity vs. Procurement Speed.
options: Perform a baseline regulatory assessment and publish findings.
Develop a regulatory roadmap that includes phased compliance milestones.
Implement a radical regulatory sandbox that allows rapid testing of platform‑neutral solutions under provisional approval.
review: Controls Regulatory Clarity vs. Procurement Speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider enforcement mechanisms.

name: EU Alignment Campaign
consequences: Immediate: Engage EU digital identity working groups → Systemic: Influence EU policy language by 20% (measured by inclusion of fallback clauses) → Strategic: Enhances Denmark’s leverage but may divert resources from national focus. Trade‑off: International Influence vs. National Resource Allocation.
options: Submit position papers to EU Digital Identity Wallet WG.
Host joint workshops with EU stakeholders to co‑create fallback standards.
Launch a radical EU‑wide digital identity consortium that mandates platform‑neutral fallback across member states.
review: Controls International Influence vs. National Resource Allocation. Weakness: The options fail to consider member state political dynamics.

name: Supplier Concentration Mitigation
consequences: Immediate: Introducing an alternative distribution clause expands the number of available app channels → Systemic: The expanded channel set increases user access by 30% and reduces supplier concentration risk by 25%, as measured by the concentration index → Strategic: This shift enhances resilience but slows deployment speed, trading rapid rollout for long‑term robustness. The trade‑off between speed and resilience is a core tension that will shape stakeholder acceptance. Measuring the 30% access increase and 25% risk reduction provides a clear KPI for monitoring progress.
options: Maintain current procurement clauses while adding a clause that requires at least one alternative distribution channel for critical apps.
Introduce a phased vendor diversification program that mandates a secondary app store partner for all future identity apps.
Deploy a decentralized, blockchain‑based app distribution network that eliminates reliance on any single commercial platform.
review: Controls supplier concentration vs. deployment speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost of maintaining multiple distribution channels.

name: Resilience‑Centric Procurement
consequences: Immediate: Adding a resilience clause to procurement specifications obliges vendors to provide fallback authentication → Systemic: This requirement increases the resilience score of the identity ecosystem by 20% and reduces downtime risk by 15%, as tracked by incident response metrics → Strategic: While this enhances system robustness, it raises procurement costs and extends contract negotiation timelines, trading cost efficiency for resilience. The tension between cost and resilience will influence vendor selection. The 20% resilience improvement and 15% downtime reduction serve as measurable KPIs.
options: Maintain current procurement guidelines while adding a resilience clause that requires fallback authentication capability.
Revise procurement specifications to mandate dual authentication pathways, ensuring at least one platform‑neutral fallback.
Introduce a dynamic procurement model that leverages micro‑licensing and real‑time risk scoring to automatically adjust vendor selection based on resilience metrics.
review: Controls cost efficiency vs. resilience. Weakness: The options ignore the potential for vendor lock‑in through proprietary fallback solutions.

name: Fallback‑Auth Standardization
consequences: Immediate: Releasing fallback authentication guidelines provides a clear reference for identity providers → Systemic: Adoption of the guidelines increases fallback readiness by 35% and reduces authentication failure incidents by 18%, as measured by audit logs → Strategic: This standardization improves system resilience but may slow integration cycles, trading speed for robustness. Balancing rapid integration with comprehensive fallback coverage is a key tension. The 35% readiness boost and 18% incident reduction are concrete KPIs.
options: Publish a set of best‑practice guidelines for fallback authentication that can be adopted by existing identity providers.
Develop a modular fallback authentication framework that can be integrated into current systems with minimal disruption.
Create a decentralized, token‑based fallback authentication protocol that operates independently of any mobile OS, leveraging zero‑knowledge proofs.
review: Controls rapid integration vs. comprehensive fallback coverage. Weakness: The options fail to account for the interoperability challenges across heterogeneous legacy systems.

name: Stakeholder‑Driven Policy Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Organizing stakeholder workshops generates a shared understanding of fallback needs → Systemic: The resulting policy draft increases stakeholder alignment by 40% and shortens the policy approval cycle by 25%, as measured by survey scores and timeline metrics → Strategic: While this accelerates policy development, it may dilute technical rigor, trading depth for breadth. The trade‑off between stakeholder inclusivity and technical precision is a central tension. The 40% alignment improvement and 25% cycle reduction provide measurable indicators.
options: Conduct a series of stakeholder workshops to gather requirements and build consensus around fallback authentication.
Launch a public consultation campaign that solicits feedback from civil society, industry, and academia on platform neutrality.
Establish a cross‑sector advisory council that uses blockchain voting to democratically decide on policy priorities.
review: Controls stakeholder inclusivity vs. technical precision. Weakness: The options overlook the influence of political lobbying on policy outcomes.

name: Cross‑Platform Trust Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Adding a federated trust model aggregates integrity signals, creating a composite trust score → Systemic: The composite score improves overall system trustworthiness by 25% and reduces single‑point failure risk by 30%, as measured by audit metrics → Strategic: This architecture enhances resilience but increases computational overhead and complexity, trading performance for robustness. Balancing performance with resilience is a key tension. The 25% trust improvement and 30% risk reduction are measurable KPIs.
options: Implement a lightweight trust anchor that relies on existing platform integrity signals without adding new dependencies.
Introduce a federated trust model that aggregates integrity signals from multiple platforms to provide a composite trust score.
Deploy a quantum‑resistant, distributed ledger‑based trust framework that eliminates reliance on any single platform's integrity API.
review: Controls performance vs. resilience. Weakness: The options ignore the potential latency impact on user authentication flows.

name: Supplier Autonomy Calibration
consequences: Immediate: Reduce reliance on single platform vendors by introducing alternative trust anchors → Systemic: Increase supplier diversity by 30% within 12 months, lowering concentration risk measured by Herfindahl index → Strategic: Enhances national resilience but may increase integration complexity, balancing autonomy against operational overhead.
options: Conduct a phased vendor diversification audit and mandate minimum two alternative platform providers for future procurements.
Implement a dual‑vendor architecture that allows switching between primary and secondary trust anchors without service interruption.
Deploy a blockchain‑based decentralized identity layer that eliminates reliance on any single commercial platform, enabling fully autonomous authentication flows.
review: Controls vendor concentration vs. integration complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost of cross‑vendor interoperability testing.

name: Resilient Access Pathway Design
consequences: Immediate: Introduce a browser‑based fallback authentication flow → Systemic: Reduce downtime risk by 40% during platform outages, measured by incident response time metrics → Strategic: Improves continuity but may dilute user experience, trading convenience against resilience.
options: Add a lightweight web‑authn fallback portal that users can access via any browser.
Integrate a hardware‑token fallback that pairs with existing mobile credentials for emergency access.
Create a quantum‑resistant, zero‑trust authentication micro‑service that operates independently of any OS, enabling instant fallback without user‑device constraints.
review: Controls convenience vs. resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider the user adoption barrier of new hardware tokens.

name: Cross‑Sector Continuity Blueprint
consequences: Immediate: Align procurement clauses across ministries to include fallback requirements → Systemic: Standardize continuity language in 80% of new contracts within 18 months, measured by clause adoption rate → Strategic: Fosters inter‑agency resilience but may slow procurement cycles, balancing uniformity against speed.
options: Draft a template procurement clause that mandates fallback authentication provisions for all digital services.
Pilot a cross‑agency continuity task force that reviews and approves fallback requirements before contract signing.
Establish a digital sovereignty marketplace where ministries can procure certified fallback solutions through a shared platform, accelerating adoption and reducing cycle time.
review: Controls procurement speed vs. continuity standardization. Weakness: The options fail to consider the budgetary impact of shared procurement infrastructure.

name: Digital Sovereignty Enforcement
consequences: Immediate: Enforce platform‑neutral access mandates in policy documents → Systemic: Increase policy compliance by 25% over 12 months, measured by audit scores → Strategic: Strengthens sovereignty but may face political pushback, balancing authority against stakeholder buy‑in.
options: Publish a mandatory policy directive that prohibits exclusive reliance on foreign platform APIs for public authentication.
Introduce a compliance certification program that rewards agencies meeting platform‑neutral criteria.
Launch a sovereign identity consortium that develops open‑source trust anchors, funded by a public‑private partnership, ensuring long‑term autonomy.
review: Controls regulatory authority vs. stakeholder buy‑in. Weakness: The options fail to consider the influence of international trade agreements.

name: Adaptive Trust Anchor Diversification
consequences: Immediate: Expand trust anchor options to include open‑source and hardware‑based solutions → Systemic: Diversify trust sources by 35% within 9 months, measured by number of distinct anchor types in use → Strategic: Enhances security posture but may increase maintenance overhead, balancing diversification against operational cost.
options: Integrate an open‑source trust anchor library into existing authentication stacks.
Deploy a hardware‑based attestation module that verifies device integrity independently of OS.
Implement a federated trust network that aggregates multiple anchor providers, leveraging AI to select the most secure path in real time.
review: Controls security posture vs. maintenance cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider the scalability of AI‑driven trust selection.

name: Stakeholder‑Led Resilience Mandate
consequences: Immediate: Convene a multi‑stakeholder council to set resilience standards → Systemic: Achieve 90% stakeholder alignment on fallback requirements within 6 months, measured by council voting outcomes → Strategic: Builds consensus but may delay decision‑making, balancing inclusivity against operational agility.
options: Form a steering committee that meets quarterly to define resilience benchmarks.
Host a national resilience summit that gathers civil society, industry, and academia to co‑create fallback guidelines.
Create a decentralized autonomous organization (DAO) that governs resilience standards through token‑based voting, ensuring continuous stakeholder engagement.
review: Controls inclusivity vs. decision‑making speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider the legal validity of DAO‑issued standards.
